content.problemStatement:
Genthru has observed that all the NNN cities on greed island have good trade relations with each other. He deduces that the reason for this is that there exists a route between any pair of cities on greed island. Being a villain he wants to sever the trade relation between at least one pair of cities, so he decides to destroy exactly 1 road among the existing MMM roads. You have to determine the exact number of roads that Genthru could destroy such that it results in there being no route between at least 1 pair of cities.

Input:
First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
Next line contains the number of cities NNN and the number of roads MMM.
Next MMM lines contain 2 integers aia_iai​ and bib_ibi​ indicating that there exists a road between aia_iai​ and bib_ibi​. (1≤i≤M1 \leq i \leq M1≤i≤M,  1≤ai≤N1 \leq a_i \leq N1≤ai​≤N, 1≤bi≤N1 \leq b_i \leq N1≤bi​≤N)
Output:For each testcase, output in a single line the number of roads that Genthru could destroy.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two integers N and M — the number of cities and the number of roads. Then M lines follow, each containing two integers a and b (1‑based) denoting an undirected road between cities a and b.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single integer — the number of roads whose removal disconnects the graph (i.e., the number of bridges). If the given graph is not connected, output 0.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First graph is connected; its bridges are edges (1,4), (4,5) and (4,6) → 3. Second graph is a triangle, no bridges → 0.
Complete graph of three vertices has no bridge.
The only edge connects the two cities; removing it disconnects the graph, so the answer is 1.

solution.approach: The graph is undirected. An edge is a bridge iff its removal increases the number of connected components. Using a single DFS we can compute discovery times (tin) and low-link values (low). For an edge (v, to) where v is the DFS parent of to, the edge is a bridge when low[to] > tin[v]. The algorithm runs DFS from every unvisited vertex, counting connected components. If the whole graph is not connected we output 0, otherwise we output the total number of bridges found. The algorithm works in O(N+M) time per test case and O(N+M) memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + M) per test case, O(total N + total M) for all test cases combined.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + M) for adjacency lists, discovery/low arrays and visited flags.

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{static int timer,bridges;static int[] tin,low;static boolean[] visited;static List<Integer>[] g;static void dfs(int v,int p){visited[v]=true;tin[v]=low[v]=++timer;for(int to:g[v]){if(to==p)continue;if(visited[to]){low[v]=Math.min(low[v],tin[to]);}else{dfs(to,v);low[v]=Math.min(low[v],low[to]);if(low[to]>tin[v])bridges++;}}}public static void main(String[]args)throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();int T=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());while(T-- >0){StringTokenizer st;do{String line=br.readLine();if(line==null)return;line=line.trim();if(!line.isEmpty()){st=new StringTokenizer(line);break;}}while(true);int N=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());int M=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());g=new ArrayList[N+1];for(int i=1;i<=N;i++)g[i]=new ArrayList<>();for(int i=0;i<M;i++){st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());int a=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());int b=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());g[a].add(b);g[b].add(a);}tin=new int[N+1];low=new int[N+1];visited=new boolean[N+1];timer=0;bridges=0;int components=0;for(int i=1;i<=N;i++)if(!visited[i]){components++;dfs(i,-1);}sb.append(components==1?bridges:0).append('\n');}System.out.print(sb.toString());}}
